does: This paper develops a systematic evaluation framework for aggregating diverse human preferences in federated reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). The authors implement a federated setting where multiple groups locally evaluate model rollouts and generate reward signals, which a central server combines without accessing raw data. They benchmark standard aggregation methods (min, max, average) against a novel adaptive scheme that dynamically weights group preferences based on historical alignment performance. Using a PPO-based RLHF pipeline on question-answering tasks, they demonstrate that their adaptive aggregation approach achieves better fairness across groups while maintaining competitive overall alignment quality, providing empirical evidence for the fairness-quality trade-off in pluralistic preference aggregation.
connects: This work builds on two intersecting research streams: federated learning approaches that preserve privacy by aggregating local updates without centralizing raw data, and the emerging problem of pluralistic or value-plural alignment where LLMs must respect multiple diverse and sometimes conflicting human value systems. It extends beyond standard RLHF reward aggregation—typically designed for single-preference scenarios—by addressing how to fairly represent groups with different preferences in federated settings. The paper responds to limitations of naive aggregation methods (mean rewards can marginalize minority preferences) and connects to fairness-in-ML literature concerned with equitable representation. It engages with recent work on value disagreement in LLM alignment and federated learning aggregation strategies, synthesizing these to handle preference diversity at scale.
enables: This framework opens paths toward more inclusive LLM deployment where multiple stakeholder groups maintain voice in model behavior without surrendering data privacy or preferences to centralized control. The adaptive weighting scheme could be extended to more sophisticated preference reconciliation methods, incorporating game-theoretic or multi-objective optimization approaches. Future work could explore whether similar aggregation principles apply to other federated learning tasks beyond RLHF, and whether the fairness metrics and trade-off analysis generalize across diverse domains and preference distributions. The systematic evaluation methodology itself provides a template for auditing alignment systems for fairness, enabling researchers and practitioners to assess whether deployed models adequately represent diverse values. This could inform governance structures for LLM development in heterogeneous populations and institutions with plural objectives.
